course_metadata.meeting_times: Lecture (Sections 509, 510, 511): Monday, Wednesday, Friday 10:20am - 11:10am; Lab (CSCE 221-509): Tuesday, Thursday 8:25am - 9:15am; Lab (CSCE 221-510): Tuesday, Thursday 9:35am - 10:25am; Lab (CSCE 221-511): Tuesday, Thursday 11:35am - 12:25pm. All meetings from 12 January 2026 - 5 May 2026.

chunks.title: Basic Course Data
chunks.content:
Course: CSCE 221 – Data Structures and Algorithms
Sections: 509 (CRN 17004), 510, 511
Term: Spring 2026 - College Station
Credit Hours: 4

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Data Structures and Algorithms. (3-2). Credit 4. Specification and implementation of basic abstract data types and their associated algorithms including stacks, queues, lists, sorting and selection, searching, graphs, and hashing; performance tradeoffs of different implementations and asymptotic analysis of running time and memory usage; includes the execution of student programs written in C++.

chunks.title: Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of C or better in CSCE 222 or ECEN 222, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:
0. Design and implement diverse data structures that allow easy access and manipulation of data using the C++ programming language.
0. Apply Big-O asymptotic notation to analyze and select an efficient algorithm for solving a given problem with respect to runtime and memory usage.

chunks.title: Recommended Textbook
chunks.content:
Title: Data Structures and Algorithm Analysis in C++
Author: Mark Allen Weiss
Edition: 4th Edition
Publisher: Addison-Wesley
ISBN: 9780132847377
Status: Recommended

chunks.title: Grade Components
chunks.content:
Homework Assignments: 9%
Programming Assignments: 24%
Lab Activities: 10%
Quizzes: 12%
Exam 1: 15%
Exam 2: 15%
Exam 3: 15%

Your final grade will be determined based on written homework, programming assignments, lab activities, quizzes, and exams.
Programming assignments will be graded on algorithm design, correct and appropriate use of data structures and/or user-defined types and their implementation, correctness, tests, and a written report describing implemented algorithms and data structures and the results of computational experiments.
All grade appeals must be made no later than one week after the grade is posted.

chunks.title: Graded Attendance
chunks.content:
Lab attendance is required and will be taken on a regular basis. Lecture attendance will be monitored via pop quizzes.
Students are responsible for learning all material covered in class, reading the assigned text from the textbook, and completing homework assignments.
Two bonus points will be added to your final score if you have a perfect lab attendance, or one point if you have only one lab absence.
Make-up exams and quizzes will only be given with documented university-approved excuses (see University Regulations and Student Rule 7). Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.

chunks.title: Notes about Graded Materials
chunks.content:
• All homework assignments will be announced in class and posted on Canvas. Deadlines will be indicated in the course calendar on Canvas.
• The written part of homework assignments and programming reports, including the cover page, should be typed, preferably using LaTeX or LaTeX-based apps (for example, Overleaf). Homework should be submitted in PDF format to Gradescope.
• All programming assignments should be implemented in C++, compiled and run on a computer (preferably using Linux), and submitted to Gradescope for grading.
• Culture assignments allow you to explore current developments in Computer Science and Engineering or learn about prominent computer scientists (e.g., Turing Award winners). These assignments should be submitted via Canvas.
• Quizzes cover material from lectures and assigned textbook reading.
• The instructor and/or TAs may request an oral exam to check a student's understanding of material covered by a test, homework, or assignment.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course-Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Programming assignments and written homework will be accepted up to one day late with a 5% penalty. Once solutions have been discussed or handed out, assignments will not be accepted.
Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor. Work submitted as makeup for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (see Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule (Tentative)
chunks.content:
This schedule is tentative and subject to change; updates will be announced in class and on Canvas.

*
Module 1- Jan 12-16 - Week 1
• Introduction to Data Structures
• Pointers, Arrays, Vectors
• Introduction to Analysis of Algorithms

*
Module 2 - Jan 19–23 – Week 2 Martin Luther King Jr. Day: Jan 19 (no classes).
• Complexity of Search and Sort Algorithms
• Algorithms - Best, Worst, Average Cases

*
Module 3- Jan 26-30 - Week 3
• The Lower Bound Theorem
• Linear Sort Algorithms
• Stack ADT - Array-based Implementation

*
Module 4-Feb 2-6 - Week 4
• Queue ADT - Array-based Implementation
• Amortized Analysis
• Stack and Queue Applications; Parser

*
Module 5- Feb 9-13 - Week 5
• Linked Lists (Stack and Queue based)
• Deques
• Recursive Algorithms
• Exam 1: Feb 9

*
Module 6 Feb 16-20 - Week 6
• Analysis of Divide-and-Conquer Algorithms
• Iteration and Master Methods

*
Module 7-Feb 23-27 - Week 7
• Binary Search Trees
• AVL Trees
• Red-Black Trees

*
Module 8 Mar 2-6- Week 8
• 2-4 Trees
• Skip Lists

*
Module 9 - Mar 16-20 – Week 10 Spring Break: Mar 9–13 (no classes).
• Hashing
• Priority Queues
• Heaps
• Exam 2: Mar 18

*
Module 10 Mar 23-27 Week 11
• Huffman Trees
• Graphs
• Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS)

*
Module 11 Mar 30-Apr 3 - Week 12
• Digraph Algorithms
• Reading Day: Apr 3 (no classes)

*
Module 12 - Apr 6-10 - Week 13
• Shortest Path Algorithms
• Dijkstra's Algorithm

*
Module 13 Apr 13-17 – Week 14
• Minimum Spanning Trees
• Prim's Algorithm
• Q-Drop Deadline: Apr 14

*
Module 14 - Apr 20-24 - Week 15
• Disjoint Set ADT

*
Module 15 Apr 27-29 – Week 16
• Kruskal's Algorithm
• Last Day of Classes: Apr 28 (Friday)
• Final Review
• Reading Day: Apr 29 (no classes)
Final Exam: Monday, May 4, 8:00am-10:00am.

chunks.title: Learning Resources and Advice
chunks.content:
This course focuses on building a strong computer science background and solid C++ programming skills. Programming is not something you can learn overnight by reading a textbook or lecture notes; it requires consistent practice.
The instructor and TAs are willing to help you learn and understand the course material and to help you improve your programming skills. A few hints for success:
• Attend class and lab meetings regularly.
• Read lecture notes and related textbook material; ask questions.
• Study for quizzes and tests.
• Retype (do not copy-paste) and implement in C++ the examples from lecture notes and the textbook.
• Complete all labs and projects.
• Visit office hours if you have questions about the course.
• Use Campuswire (or other designated discussion platform) to ask questions and participate in discussions.
In general, Computer Science is not an easy subject, but it will pay off after graduation.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves, although discussion with TAs, peer teachers, and the instructor is allowed.
Students must not have another person or entity do the writing of any portion of an assignment for them. This includes hiring a person or company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools such as ChatGPT for graded work.

chunks.title: Technology Support
chunks.content: For technology support, students should consult the university IT resources and Canvas support services (see the official course Canvas page for up-to-date contact information).

chunks.title: Learning Resources (Campuswire and Peer Teachers)
chunks.content:
Campuswire (or the designated online platform) will serve as the consolidated and customizable home base for course communication. It provides Q&A forums and chatrooms for group discussion.
Peer Teachers (PTs) help students understand material from classes and labs, practice computational thinking, and improve programming skills. See course materials for current PT locations and schedules.
